did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically ResNeXt50, to analyze histologic whole slide images (WSIs) for the binary classification of melanoma versus nevus in a dermatopathology context. It also employed a Random Forest ensemble learning method as a patient data classifier and investigated fusion techniques combining CNN image features with patient data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'Original Research' on the first page. It presents original experimental results from a study combining CNN-based histologic whole slide image analysis with patient data for skin cancer classification, including specific methods, datasets (431 WSIs), and quantitative results (AUROC, balanced accuracy).

title: Combining CNN-based histologic whole slide image analysis and patient data to improve skin cancer classification
brief_summary: This study investigated whether combining histologic whole slide image (WSI) analysis with patient data improves the binary classification of melanoma versus nevus. The researchers analyzed 431 H&E-stained WSIs from two laboratories using a ResNeXt50 convolutional neural network for image features and a Random Forest classifier for patient data (age, sex, anatomical site). They evaluated three fusion techniques and a naive strategy where patient data replaced the CNN decision for cases with low confidence scores (output <0.7). The CNN alone achieved the highest performance with an AUROC of 92.30% and balanced accuracy of 83.17%. While general fusion did not significantly improve performance, the naive strategy improved balanced accuracy to 86.72% for low-confidence cases. The authors concluded that while patient data integration generally offered no benefit due to the CNN's high accuracy, it was useful for correcting uncertain predictions.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. Included 66% of melanomas had a tumour thickness of 1 mm or less. Total count: 209 slides (111 training, 33 validation, 65 test).

One of the two classes in the binary classification task. Included 54% moderate to severely dysplastic nevi, 42% not or weakly dysplastic nevi, and several blue, spitzoid and Reed nevi. Total count: 222 slides (121 training, 34 validation, 67 test).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of melanoma versus nevus
all_task_types.details: The study performs binary classification of histologic whole slide images (WSIs) to distinguish between melanoma and nevus. The primary model (ResNeXt50 CNN) outputs class probabilities/scores for this binary decision. A secondary Random Forest classifier also performs binary classification using patient data (age, sex, anatomical site). Performance is measured using AUROC and balanced accuracy.

datasets_used.name: Retrospective Multi-Center H&E-Stained WSI Cohort
datasets_used.description: A collection of 431 H&E-stained histologic whole slide images (WSIs) corresponding to 430 patients, retrospectively collected from two different laboratories (a private dermatopathology laboratory in Heidelberg, Germany, and an anatomic pathology unit in Macerata, Italy). The dataset includes patient metadata (age, sex, anatomical site) and diagnoses made by experienced pathologists.

use.training: Used to train the ResNeXt50 CNN image classifier and the Random Forest patient data classifier. Tiles were limited to 100 per slide to avoid class imbalance.
use.validation: Used to validate the models. All tiles of the WSI were used during validation.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final performance of the classifiers and fusion strategies. All tiles of the WSI were used during testing.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: area under the receiver operating characteristic

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure the performance (AUROC and balanced accuracy) of five identically trained models on the test set.
To test for significant correlations between patient age and diagnosis.
To test for significant correlations between sex, anatomical site, and diagnosis.
Used instead of the Chi-squared test for small sample sizes to test for significant correlations between sex, anatomical site, and diagnosis.
To split the dataset into training, validation, and test sets while taking diagnosis, patient data, and laboratory into account.
Predefined threshold for statistical significance in hypothesis tests.

hypothesis_testing.test: Mann-Whitney rank-sum test, Chi-squared test, Fisher’s exact test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant correlation between the type of patient data (age, sex, anatomical site) and the diagnosis (melanoma vs. nevus).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant correlation between the type of patient data (age, sex, anatomical site) and the diagnosis (melanoma vs. nevus).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Mann-Whitney rank-sum test was used for patient age, and Chi-squared or Fisher’s exact tests were used for sex and anatomical site. Results showed that patient age correlated significantly with diagnosis (P < 0.001 in training/test sets). Anatomical location 'head & neck' correlated significantly in training and test sets (P = 0.002 and P < 0.001, respectively). Sex did not correlate significantly with diagnosis (P = 0.119 in test set).

Retrospective Multi-Center H&E-Stained W.notes: The CNN alone achieved the best performance. General fusion techniques did not significantly improve performance compared to the CNN alone. The naive approach, which replaced the CNN decision with the patient data classifier decision for cases with low confidence (CNN output score < 0.7), improved balanced accuracy to 86.72% but decreased AUROC to 89.30%. The patient data classifier alone performed significantly worse than the CNN. Statistical significance was determined using Mann-Whitney rank-sum test, Chi-squared test, and Fisher’s exact test with P < 0.05.

architecture_used.input_size: 512x512 pixels (tiles extracted from Whole Slide Images)

fusion_models.name: Weighted Approach
fusion_models.details: Two paths: CNN for image tiles and a second neural network for patient data. Each path has its own linear classifying layer outputting a score. Scores are combined with weights.

fusion_models.name: CAT-approach (Concatenation)
fusion_models.details: Concatenation of the CNN feature vector and the patient data feature vector before the final classifying layer.

fusion_models.name: SE-approach (Squeeze-and-Excitation)
fusion_models.details: Patient data features are used to scale the CNN features in Squeeze-and-Excitation style before the final classifying layer.

architecture_details.secondary_classifier: Random Forest ensemble learning method (used for patient data classification)

training_details.tiles_per_slide_limit: 100 randomly picked tiles per slide during training to avoid class imbalance; all tiles used during validation and testing
training_details.background_discard: Tiles with more than 50% background pixels (luma >220) were discarded

classification_or_output_head.naive_approach_threshold: 0.7 (CNN output score threshold for replacing CNN decision with patient data classifier decision)

preprocessing_techniques: Patient data encoding: Age encoded numerically; Sex encoded binarily (0=female, 1=male); Anatomical site grouped into three categories (extremity, head & neck, trunk) and encoded (0, 1, 2).
Patient data imputation: Missing patient data values replaced by mean age or most frequent sex and anatomical site within the dataset.
Image annotation: WSIs annotated by an experienced dermatopathologist to select regions of interest.
Tile extraction: Regions of interest partitioned into 512x512 pixel tiles using QuPath.
Background filtering: Tiles with more than 50% background pixels (luma >220) were discarded.
Tile sampling limit: Maximum of 100 randomly picked tiles per slide during training to avoid class imbalance; all tiles used during validation and testing.
conclusions: The CNN (ResNeXt50) alone achieved the highest performance for binary melanoma/nevus classification, with an AUROC of 92.30% and balanced accuracy of 83.17%.
General integration of patient data (age, sex, anatomical site) via fusion techniques did not significantly improve classification performance compared to the CNN alone.
The patient data classifier (Random Forest) performed significantly worse than the CNN alone (AUROC 76.97% vs 92.30%).

A 'naive approach' that replaced the CNN decision with the patient data classifier decision for cases with low confidence (CNN output score < 0.7) improved balanced accuracy to 86.72% for those specific cases, although it decreased the overall AUROC to 89.30%.
Patient data integration is most beneficial for correcting uncertain predictions where the CNN has low confidence, rather than improving overall accuracy when the CNN is already highly accurate.
The study is limited by the relatively small number of included cases (431 WSIs), which may affect the statistical significance of correlations in validation and test sets.
The study was limited to commonly available patient data; other clinical information (e.g., phototype, family history) might be more relevant.
Larger studies and validation in different environments (e.g., different laboratories or patient populations) are needed to confirm these findings and facilitate clinical translation.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a relatively small sample size (431 WSIs), which may affect the statistical significance of correlations in validation and test sets.
The dataset was restricted to commonly available patient data (age, sex, anatomical site); other potentially relevant clinical information (e.g., phototype, family history) was not included.
The study lacks external validation in different environments, such as slides from different laboratories or distinct patient populations, which is necessary for clinical translation.

General fusion techniques did not significantly improve performance compared to the CNN alone, suggesting that patient data integration offers limited benefit when the image classifier is already highly accurate.

The naive strategy improved balanced accuracy for low-confidence cases but decreased the overall AUROC, indicating a trade-off between sensitivity for uncertain cases and overall discriminative ability.
